upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a shallow, stable boundary layer with a very weak surface wind (305°/4 kts) and a pronounced inversion evident from the surface-to-850mb lapse rate of only -4.39°C/km — well below dry adiabatic, indicating strong capping near the surface. The 850mb level shows an anomalously warm 18°C with a dew point spread of 49°C, confirming a robust subsidence inversion likely sitting around 1,500m MSL that will hard-cap any thermal development. Upper-level winds at 700mb are light at 16 kts from 260° and 500mb at 21 kts from 275°, indicating moderate westerly flow aloft with no significant wind shear hazard but confirming the synoptic sea-breeze pattern dominating the surface.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for Bay Area. KHAF reporting mist/BR at 2.5–3SM visibility with 320-340°/8-10 kts — marine layer lingering at the coast through at least mid-afternoon. KSFO calm with FEW200, no low cloud at the bay. KRHV calm/light northerly with SKC at 16–18°C, clean inland conditions. KAPC and KWVI both calm with CLR — no notable anomalies inland. Buoys HMB 46012 and SF Bar 46026 showing 13-16 kts from 310-320° with SST 12.2-12.6°C, cold enough to support a persistent marine layer at the coast.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 is primarily a thermal site with ridge-assist; the launch faces roughly southwest and needs a NW-to-W sea breeze or light NE morning flow to be viable. Ideal conditions are 8–15 kts on-ridge with a thermal ceiling allowing meaningful altitude gain above launch.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor against flyable conditions at the 1750 launch this morning is the strong NNE gradient wind — 10–13 kts at 10m from 22–36° through late morning — which is cross to off for this SW-facing launch and would create rotor and turbulent conditions on the ridge. The KOAK sounding's shallow inversion and low BL height (120–350m above ground) confirms thermal ceilings are minimal all day. By early afternoon the surface wind backs toward 345–360° and lightens to 6–8 kts, then shifts to NW/WNW (305–345°) by mid-afternoon at 5–7 kts — more workable directions — but the BL height never exceeds 405m AGL and CAPE is zero throughout, meaning thermal support is essentially absent. Tomorrow.io gusts to 15 kts are noted in the early afternoon window, flagging punchy potential even in the lighter period. The cloud spike to 97% at 1600 is suspicious and likely a model artifact, but overall the story is: wrong wind direction in the morning, thermally capped and gusty in the afternoon window — no viable launch opportunity today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10–13 kts from NNE (22–36°) — off-the-back for the SW launch. BL height only 120–350m. Not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind backing toward 345–360° and lightening to 7–10 kts; near-north flow is still awkward for the SW launch and BL height remains under 380m with zero thermals.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts to 305–345° NW at 5–7 kts — better direction but gusts to 15 kts (Tomorrow.io) suggest punchy air; BL height still only 380–405m and capped, not worth it.

verdict.wind: 10–13 kts NNE morning, shifting NW 5–7 kts PM
verdict.hazards: Off-the-back NNE gradient wind morning, subsidence inversion caps thermals all day, punchy afternoon gusts to 15 kts

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, W-facing slope
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is a short training/sled-ride hill that works on light westerly to NW flow, primarily for P2 training flights and short ridge soaring. It needs gentle 8–12 kts on-face from the W-NW; strong or off-direction flow makes it unsuitable.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch shares the same adverse morning gradient as the upper launch — 9–10 kts from NNE (2–21°) through 10 AM, which is off-the-back for this W-facing hill. The wind backs and lightens significantly by midday to 4–5 kts from 311–340° (NW/WNW), which is the correct direction for this face, but the speeds are borderline light for meaningful ridge lift. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 11–13 kts at the lower elevation in the afternoon, which while manageable for a P3 is punchy for a P2 training environment. BL heights of 330–440m AGL indicate thermals are very weak but present by midday — acceptable for a sled ride but not for soaring. The overall picture is a possible short afternoon window for P3 pilots but not suitable for P2 training given the gusty character.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 9–10 kts from NNE — wrong direction for W-facing launch. Not usable for any level.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind backs to 311–340° NW at 4–5 kts — correct direction but very light. Sled rides possible for P3 in calm air; BL height 365–410m.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 5–8 kts from WNW/NW with gusts to 11–13 kts (Tomorrow.io) — gusty and punchy; P3 only with caution.

verdict.hazards: Off-the-back NNE flow until ~11 AM, afternoon gusts to 13 kts, very low thermal ceiling (~400m AGL)

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft training hill is the mellowest Ed Levin launch, used almost exclusively for beginner/P1-P2 ground handling and short sled rides. It needs gentle westerly flow under 10 kts on-face; even minor turbulence or off-direction flow makes it inappropriate for new pilots.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft hill sees different wind behavior than the upper launches this morning — the low-level flow here starts from W-WNW (245–297°) at 2–5 kts, which is actually on-face and gentle enough for early morning beginner use. However, the BL height is only 80m at 8 AM, meaning essentially no thermal development and very short flights. Through the day, the wind remains westerly at 5–7 kts from 270–293° — the correct direction for this W-facing hill — and is generally the most consistent and flyable of the three Ed Levin launches today. The afternoon sees gusts to 11–13 kts per Tomorrow.io, which is at the upper limit for a beginner training hill. A brief usable window exists mid-morning to early afternoon before afternoon gustiness develops.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light W-WNW 2–6 kts from 245–270° — on-face and gentle, suitable for beginner training/sled rides. BL height 80–325m.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 5–7 kts from 270–283° W — steady on-face flow, best window of the day. BL height 325–375m allows short thermal assist.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind picks up to 7–8 kts from WNW/NW with gusts to 11–13 kts (Tomorrow.io) — borderline for beginner terrain; P2 caution advised.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon gusts to 13 kts per Tomorrow.io, very shallow thermal ceiling, not suitable for soaring — sled rides only

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge lift site driven by the Pacific sea breeze and onshore flow over the coastal bluff. It needs 10–20 kts from the W-WSW to W-NW for sustained soaring; too light and it's a sled ride, too strong and rotor/turbulence below the bluff becomes a hazard. The marine layer burn-off timing is critical.
sites.todaySetup: The marine layer is the story at Mussel Rock today, and it's working against an early start. KHAF is reporting mist and only 2.5–3SM visibility with 320-340°/8-10 kts as of mid-afternoon — marine layer is deep and persistent at the coast. Buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) shows SST of 12.6°C, cold enough to anchor a robust marine layer, with 15.6 kts from 310° confirming the sea breeze machine is running but with the layer still intact. GFS/HRRR both show surface winds at Mussel Rock slowly ramping from 3–4 kts (light, southwesterly) in the morning to 7–9 kts from 265–282° (W/WSW) by 2–4 PM — adequate but on the lighter side for sustained soaring. The wind speeds in the 1–5 PM window are within the acceptable range (7–9 kts), but the marine cloud layer persists (100% cloud noted at 1500 in the model) and visibility at the coast remains reduced. Tomorrow.io actually shows lighter winds (2–5 kts) through the evening, disagreeing with HRRR's 8–9 kts — given the KHAF METAR showing persistent marine influence, the truth is likely somewhere in between, favoring a marginal afternoon window if the layer burns back enough. The short window between 1–5 PM offers the best chance with 7–9 kts onshore.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine layer limiting: 3–4 kts from S-SW, light and variable — insufficient for ridge soaring. KHAF still reporting mist and reduced visibility.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind building to 5–7 kts from 265–273° W; still marginal speed for lift, marine layer potentially thinning but cloud cover at 100% per model. Watchful waiting.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best shot: 7–9 kts from 265–282° W/WSW by 2–5 PM if marine layer recedes; Tomorrow.io and HRRR disagree on strength so conditions could be lighter. Fly with conservative exit plan.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer with mist and reduced visibility persisting at coast (KHAF), cold SST anchoring layer, wind speeds marginal for lift, model disagreement on afternoon speeds

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak, SW-facing launch, thermal and ridge hybrid
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a hybrid thermal and ridge site with a SW-facing launch. It works best on NW-to-W sea breeze in the 8–15 kt range that creates ridge lift on the face while the inland heating drives thermal activity. Strong NE gradient winds are off-the-back and dangerous.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same morning problem as the upper Ed Levin launches: a strong NNE gradient wind of 11–13 kts from 22–36° through mid-morning, which is directly off-the-back for the SW launch and creates rotor risk on the lee side. The KOAK sounding confirms the inversion is capping thermals effectively, with a paltry -4.39°C/km lapse rate and BL heights starting at just 120m. By early afternoon the wind backs to 345–360° (north) at 6–8 kts and then shifts to 305–337° WNW/NW at 5–7 kts by 2–4 PM, which approaches workable direction for the SW launch — but thermal ceilings remain suppressed at 380–405m AGL with zero CAPE. The 120m level Wind80m data shows elevated flow (17–18 kts NNE in the morning) that is well above surface wind, suggesting strong wind shear in the low BL. Tomorrow.io gusts of 10–12 kts in the afternoon with N-NNE direction (3–5°) don't align with the NW shift seen in HRRR — this directional disagreement adds uncertainty. Overall: off-the-back in the morning, marginally better direction in the afternoon but thermally capped and uncertain — not worth launching today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 11–13 kts from NNE (22–36°) — directly off-the-back for SW launch. Strong 80m wind of 17 kts confirms deep gradient. Do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind backs to 345–360° N and lightens to 6–8 kts — still cross/off for SW launch; BL height 370–380m, zero thermals. No window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Shifts to 305–337° WNW/NW at 5–7 kts — marginally better direction but gusty to 10–12 kts per Tomorrow.io and thermally capped; not viable.

verdict.wind: 11–13 kts NNE morning, shifting NW 5–7 kts PM
verdict.hazards: Off-the-back NNE gradient wind all morning, strong wind shear (17 kts at 80m vs 11 kts surface), subsidence inversion kills thermals, afternoon direction uncertainty

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday shows 100% cloud cover all morning and 1% precip — marine layer or overcast likely blanketing the site. BL height only 60m at 8 AM. Wind is light NNE/N 4–6 kts, improving in direction but the overcast kills thermals. Afternoon may partially clear with WNW 8 kts by 4 PM. Marginal at best for late afternoon, not recommended.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same marine layer/overcast story as 1750 in the morning. Light NW flow 6–8 kts afternoon with partial clearing possible. Marginal afternoon window if skies clear after 2 PM, but 100% cloud cover through midday is a wash.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Morning overcast 96–100% cloud through noon. Light WNW 4–7 kts afternoon. If the cloud breaks after 2 PM as models suggest (dropping to 16–25%), a short training window may open. Low confidence given cloud persistence.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow looks unfavorable — 100% cloud cover all day per HRRR with 1–3% precip chance. Wind 7–10 kts WNW which would be adequate, but overcast all day kills the experience and light drizzle risk is present. Tomorrow.io shows 21 kts gusts at 8 AM — potentially strong/gusty. No fly recommended.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: 100% overcast morning, 1% precip, BL height only 60m at 8 AM. Wind light and variable NNE through midday, partial clearing possibly by 2–4 PM. Same marginal-at-best story as Ed Levin 1750. Not recommended.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (Mar 24) looks significantly better — clear skies, calm morning, building WNW 5–7 kts by noon with BL height reaching 500–735m by early afternoon. CAPE still zero but the higher BL height and clear skies allow for much better thermal soaring. Best day of the extended outlook.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday mirrors the 1750 story — clear, calm morning transitioning to WNW 5–7 kts with BL heights to 735m. Good afternoon window shaping up for all skill levels.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday: calm morning, then WNW 5–7 kts with BL heights 500–735m by afternoon. Solid training day with potential for longer soaring flights from 300 ft.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday looks clean — calm morning, then WNW 5–6 kts building to 8–10 kts by 4 PM from 283–299°. Cloud-free. A solid ridge day if afternoon sea breeze delivers expected speeds. Watch for it building stronger toward evening.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows calm morning then WNW developing to 4–5 kts at surface with 80m winds 7–11 kts by afternoon. BL heights reach 510–735m. Better thermal ceiling and clean skies make this a viable afternoon fly day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday (Mar 25) continues the good pattern — clear skies, BL heights 575–855m, WNW 5–9 kts. Potentially the strongest thermal day of the 4-day window. Worth planning for.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday: same strong pattern as 1750 with BL heights 575–855m and NW 5–9 kts. Good soaring day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday mirrors the upper launches — clean, BL heights to 855m, NW 5–9 kts. Could be a good XC or extended soaring day from the lower launch.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Wednesday ramps up significantly — 12–13 kts from 289–291° by afternoon, 80m winds 10–12 kts. Could be a strong ridge day but winds approach the upper acceptable range. Watch for overpowering conditions; P3+ only.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Wednesday looks like the best thermal day of the week — BL heights 565–745m, WNW 3–5 kts surface, 80m winds 8–11 kts. Zero CAPE still but the deeper BL should allow meaningful thermal soaring above the ridge.

watchlist: Marine layer anchored by cold SST (12.2–12.6°C) at the coast — KHAF mist and 2.5–3SM visibility persisting through afternoon; Mussel Rock burn-off timing highly uncertain
Strong NNE gradient wind at Ed Levin and Mission Peak through mid-morning creates off-the-back and rotor conditions — do not launch at SW-facing sites before wind backs NW
Model disagreement: Tomorrow.io shows 2–5 kts at Mussel Rock PM vs HRRR 8–9 kts — actual sea breeze strength at the coast is uncertain today
Tomorrow (Monday) overcast all day at all sites with possible light drizzle — rest day likely; Tuesday shaping up as the best fly day of the week
Subsidence inversion from KOAK sounding (-4.39°C/km lapse rate, 18°C at 850mb) hard-caps thermals at all sites today — thermal soaring not viable
tldr: Today's best bet is a marginal afternoon window at Mussel Rock (2–5 PM, 7–9 kts W/WSW) if the marine layer recedes, or a short midday window at Ed Levin 300 ft for sled rides. Ed Levin upper and Mission Peak are off-the-back all morning with strong NNE gradient wind making them no-fly. If you can wait, Tuesday looks like the real fly day — clear skies, BL heights to 735m, and proper WNW flow at all sites.
